title: Tax Lobbying, Corporate Tax Avoidance, and the Political Money Behind Tax Policy

tcja_2017.overview: The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA), signed December 22, 2017, was the largest overhaul of the U.S. tax code in 30+ years. Its centerpiece — slashing the corporate rate from 35% to 21% — was the product of an intense corporate lobbying campaign. The law overwhelmingly benefited corporations and wealthy individuals while adding $1.5 trillion to the deficit over a decade.

revenue_cost.jct_10_year_estimate: $1.456 trillion added to annual deficits (debt) over ten years
revenue_cost.source: Joint Committee on Taxation / Congressional Budget Office

corporate_rate_cut.effective_rate_reality: 379 profitable Fortune 500 corporations paid an effective rate of 11.3% in 2018 — slightly more than half the statutory 21%
corporate_rate_cut.corporate_revenue_loss: $919 billion in reduced revenues projected FY2018-2027

lobbying_during_passage.q4_2017_lobbying_surge: Lobby disclosure reports listing taxation as an issue jumped from a typical ~3,000/quarter to 4,673 in Q4 2017

top_three_spenders_q4_2017.us_chamber_of_commerce: Part of $56M+ combined with NAR and Business Roundtable in Q4 2017
top_three_spenders_q4_2017.business_roundtable: $17.3 million in Q4 2017 alone — nearly quadruple the previous quarter's spending

lobbying_during_passage.beer_institute_example: $1.3 million in Q4 2017 lobbying — a $390,000 increase over prior quarter

distribution_of_benefits.top_1_percent: 47.6-62.6% of total benefits depending on version
distribution_of_benefits.top_0_1_percent: 83.7% of households in top 0.1% received average tax cut of $193,380
distribution_of_benefits.top_vs_bottom: Top 1% received ~$60,000 benefit vs $500 for bottom 60%
distribution_of_benefits.source: Economic Policy Institute / Center for American Progress

stock_buybacks_after_passage.increase_from_2017: 55% jump over 2017; 88% increase by some measures
stock_buybacks_after_passage.top_15_cash_holders: Buybacks increased from $86 billion in 2017 to $231 billion in 2018
stock_buybacks_after_passage.worker_bonuses_comparison: $6 billion in worker bonuses vs $171 billion in buybacks (by February 2018)
stock_buybacks_after_passage.capex_share: Only ~20% of incremental cash went to capital expenditure or R&D; rest went to buybacks, dividends

offshore_profits_tax_holiday.amount_held_offshore_pre_tcja: $2.6 trillion held offshore by Fortune 500 companies (2017)
offshore_profits_tax_holiday.tcja_repatriation_rate: 15.5% on cash holdings, 8% on non-cash assets — massive discount from 35%
offshore_profits_tax_holiday.result: Repatriated earnings flowed primarily into stock buybacks, not job creation

2025_extension_battle.expiring_provisions: Individual rate cuts, pass-through deduction, higher estate exemption, and several business provisions set to expire end of 2025
2025_extension_battle.resolution: One Big Beautiful Bill Act signed July 4, 2025 — made individual TCJA provisions permanent, restored 100% bonus depreciation, raised SALT cap to $40,000
2025_extension_battle.source: Bipartisan Policy Center / Joint Committee on Taxation

corporate_tax_avoidance.overview: Despite a statutory corporate tax rate of 21% (down from 35%), hundreds of major corporations pay far less — or nothing at all — through legal tax avoidance strategies. The gap between statutory and effective rates costs the U.S. government hundreds of billions annually.

companies_paying_zero_federal_tax.2018_count: 91 profitable Fortune 500 companies paid $0 on combined profits — including Amazon, Chevron, Halliburton, and IBM
companies_paying_zero_federal_tax.2020_count: 55 profitable corporations paid $0 on $40 billion in combined profits
companies_paying_zero_federal_tax.2020_total_drain: $12 billion drained from U.S. government (avoided tax + rebates)
companies_paying_zero_federal_tax.2018_2020_sustained_zero: 26 companies paid nothing over the entire three-year period 2018-2020
companies_paying_zero_federal_tax.2021_count: 19 Fortune 100 companies paid zero or near-zero

notable_zero_tax_companies.source: ITEP / CNBC / Center for American Progress

effective_vs_statutory_rates.tax_savings_from_rate_drop: $240 billion less paid 2018-2021 compared to pre-TCJA effective rates
effective_vs_statutory_rates.profits_sheltered: 39% of corporate income sheltered from the corporate income tax during 2018-2021
effective_vs_statutory_rates.gao_finding: 34% of large, profitable corporations pay zero in federal income taxes

annual_revenue_lost.overall_tax_gap: $600 billion annually (Treasury estimate); IRS projects $540 billion/year for TY2017-2019
annual_revenue_lost.offshore_profit_shifting_loss: ~$150 billion/year in federal and state revenue
annual_revenue_lost.top_1_percent_noncompliance: $175 billion/year lost to tax noncompliance by richest 1%

political_spending_by_zero_tax_companies.total_political_spending: $450 million in lobbying and campaign contributions (2015-2020) by the 55 companies paying $0 in 2020
political_spending_by_zero_tax_companies.top_spender_fedex: $71 million on lobbying and campaign contributions (2016-2020 cycles)
political_spending_by_zero_tax_companies.tcja_lobbying: 22 of the 55 companies lobbied specifically on the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act

tax_lobbying_totals.overview: Tax policy consistently ranks as the most heavily lobbied issue in Washington. Nearly half of all federal lobbyists work on tax issues, and spending surges during major reform efforts.

annual_tax_lobbying.lobbyists_on_tax_issues_2024: 6,107 federal lobbyists lobbied on tax issues
annual_tax_lobbying.percentage_of_all_lobbyists: 47% of all federal lobbyists lobbied on tax issues in 2024

top_overall_lobbying_spenders.us_chamber_tax_lobbyists_2024: 99 tax-issue lobbyists — more than any other entity

business_roundtable.q4_2017_spending: $17.3 million — nearly quadruple the prior quarter
business_roundtable.q4_2017_outside_lobbying: Nearly $500,000 paid to outside lobbying organizations
business_roundtable.activity: Lobbied specifically on TCJA provisions and 2025 extension

tcja_specific_lobbying.q4_2017_combined_top_3: $56 million+ combined (US Chamber, NAR, Business Roundtable) in Q4 2017
tcja_specific_lobbying.lobby_filings_spike: From typical ~3,000 tax filings/quarter to 4,673 in Q4 2017

lobbying_roi.study_detail: 93 firms spent $282.7 million lobbying for a 2004 'tax holiday' and saved $62.5 billion total
lobbying_roi.broader_finding: Companies that lobby more on taxes consistently pay lower effective tax rates
lobbying_roi.fixed_fortunes: Biggest corporate political interests spend billions, get trillions in benefits

carried_interest_loophole.overview: The carried interest loophole allows private equity and hedge fund managers to pay the 20% capital gains rate instead of the 37% ordinary income rate on their management fees. Despite bipartisan criticism from Obama, Trump, Clinton, Sanders, and Biden, the loophole has survived every attempt to close it — a testament to the financial industry's lobbying power.

mechanics.who_benefits: Private equity, hedge fund, and venture capital fund managers
mechanics.tcja_change: Only tightened holding period from 1 year to 3 years — loophole preserved

annual_cost.estimated_range: $2 billion to $18 billion per year depending on methodology
annual_cost.cbo_10_year_estimate: $14 billion over 10 years (2017 estimate for closing the loophole)
annual_cost.ira_removal_cost: $17 billion that would have been raised under Inflation Reduction Act provision
annual_cost.source: Congressional Budget Office / Congressional Research Service

attempts_to_close.obama_budgets: Called for carried interest reform in multiple budget proposals — blocked by Congress
attempts_to_close.trump_2016_campaign: Pledged to close the loophole; TCJA only tightened holding period to 3 years
attempts_to_close.2017_tcja: Loophole preserved; only minor holding period extension
attempts_to_close.2021_build_back_better: Included carried interest reform — bill stalled
attempts_to_close.2022_inflation_reduction_act: Provision to close loophole removed at Sinema's insistence
attempts_to_close.2025_one_big_beautiful_bill: Carried interest loophole left intact again despite Trump's earlier rhetoric

sinema_private_equity_connection.wall_street_donations_since_2018: $2 million+ from securities and investment industry
sinema_private_equity_connection.blackstone_donations: $44,000 from Blackstone employees, executives, and family members since 2018
sinema_private_equity_connection.kkr_carlyle_apollo_combined: $95,000+ combined from KKR, Carlyle Group, and Apollo Global Management (2018-2022 cycle)
sinema_private_equity_connection.ira_demand: Sinema demanded removal of carried interest reform as condition for her vote on the Inflation Reduction Act
sinema_private_equity_connection.schumer_quote: Senate Majority Leader Chuck Schumer said Democrats had 'no choice' but to drop the carried interest provision

private_equity_lobbying.american_investment_council: Primary PE lobbying arm — paid $100,000 to The Duberstein Group for carried interest lobbying during TCJA
private_equity_lobbying.blackstone_lobbying_surge_2010: Increased lobbying budget from $6.7M to $9M when Obama proposed closing the loophole
private_equity_lobbying.pe_industry_total_2017: $1 million+ spent on lobbying by PE industry during TCJA, including $600,000+ from the American Investment Council
private_equity_lobbying.blackstone_carlyle_kkr_gop_donations_201: $1.31 million combined to GOP lawmakers in 2017 alone vs $438,000 to Democrats
private_equity_lobbying.blackstone_to_mcconnell_2017: $212,000 from Blackstone employees to Mitch McConnell
private_equity_lobbying.blackstone_2020_cycle: $43 million in total contributions to federal campaigns

pe_hedge_fund_total_political_spending.source: Americans for Financial Reform / CNBC / OpenSecrets

offshore_tax_havens.overview: U.S. multinational corporations shift hundreds of billions in profits to low-tax jurisdictions through complex structures, costing the U.S. an estimated $150 billion/year in lost revenue. The Netherlands, Ireland, and Bermuda alone account for 35% of all overseas profits reported by U.S. multinationals, despite holding less than 0.3% of the world's population.

corporate_inversions.first_wave_1996_2004: Mainly 'naked inversions' to Caribbean tax havens (Bermuda, Cayman Islands) — ended by 2004 ACJA/IRS Section 7874
corporate_inversions.second_wave_2012_2016: Mainly to OECD havens: Ireland and the United Kingdom — involved mergers with real companies
corporate_inversions.largest_completed_inversion: Medtronic-Covidien: $48 billion merger in Ireland (2015) — the vast majority of revenues still from the U.S.; driven by $20 billion in untaxed offshore reserves
corporate_inversions.largest_aborted_inversion: Pfizer-Allergan: $160 billion merger (2016) — would have avoided $40.7 billion in taxes on offshore earnings; blocked by Obama Treasury anti-inversion rules

apple_ireland.effective_tax_rate_in_ireland: As low as 0.005% — despite Ireland's statutory 12.5% rate
apple_ireland.eu_commission_ruling_2016: Ordered Apple to pay €13 billion + interest in unpaid Irish taxes — largest corporate tax fine in history
apple_ireland.paradise_papers_revelation: After Ireland closed loopholes, Apple moved IP holdings to Jersey (Channel Islands)

google_double_irish_dutch_sandwich.profits_shifted_2017: $23 billion routed through Ireland and Netherlands to Bermuda
google_double_irish_dutch_sandwich.structure: Double Irish with Dutch Sandwich — profits flow through Irish subsidiary → Dutch subsidiary → Bermuda holding company
google_double_irish_dutch_sandwich.double_irish_closed: Ireland phased out the Double Irish arrangement; existing structures had until 2020 to close

fortune_500_offshore_holdings.top_holders: Apple, Microsoft, Pfizer, GE, and IBM among the largest offshore cash holders
fortune_500_offshore_holdings.subsidiaries_in_havens: 366 Fortune 500 companies maintained 9,755 subsidiaries in tax havens (2017)

netherlands_as_conduit.eu_revenue_loss: $10 billion/year in lost corporate tax across EU countries
netherlands_as_conduit.role: Primary conduit for routing profits to zero-tax jurisdictions through holding company structures

global_minimum_tax_15_percent.agreement: 140+ countries signed OECD agreement for 15% minimum corporate tax rate
global_minimum_tax_15_percent.scope: Companies with earnings above €750 million ($812 million)
global_minimum_tax_15_percent.implementation: Income Inclusion Rule took effect January 2024; ~90% of in-scope multinationals subject to it by 2025
global_minimum_tax_15_percent.expected_impact: OECD estimates profit shifting will fall by half
global_minimum_tax_15_percent.us_position: Trump pulled U.S. out of implementation framework; January 2026 'Side-by-Side' agreement effectively excludes U.S. multinationals from the 15% global minimum
global_minimum_tax_15_percent.criticism: 15% rate criticized as too low; numerous exceptions and carve-outs undermine effectiveness; detrimental to developing economies

wealth_and_estate_tax.overview: Wealthy families have spent decades and millions of dollars lobbying to weaken or eliminate the estate tax. The rebranding of the 'estate tax' as the 'death tax' by Republican strategist Frank Luntz in the 1990s was one of the most effective framing operations in modern political history.

death_tax_rebranding.architect: Frank Luntz — Republican pollster and messaging strategist
death_tax_rebranding.year: 1995 — Luntz poll-tested the term 'death tax' and advised the new GOP majority to never use 'inheritance' or 'estate tax' again
death_tax_rebranding.effectiveness: Shifted public framing from a tax on wealthy 'estates' to a universal tax on 'death' — despite only ~2 in 1,000 decedents being subject to it

key_families_funding_repeal.report: 2006 United for a Fair Economy report identified 18 families financing coordinated repeal campaign

death_tax_rebranding.patricia_soldano: Estate planner who launched repeal effort with ~50 wealthy clients; her Policy and Taxation Group spent $250,000+/year on lobbying
death_tax_rebranding.life_insurance_lobby: Coalition for America's Priorities spent ~$1.5 million fighting to preserve the estate tax (insurance industry benefits from estate tax planning)
death_tax_rebranding.source: CNN Money / The Daily Beast / Brookings

estate_tax_exemption_history.1916_creation: $50,000 exemption (~$1.5 million in today's dollars); top rate 10%
estate_tax_exemption_history.fdr_era: Top rate raised to 77% on estates over $10 million
estate_tax_exemption_history.tcja_2018_doubling: Doubled to ~$11.18 million individual / $22.36 million couple
estate_tax_exemption_history.obbba_extension: One Big Beautiful Bill Act made higher exemption permanent at $15 million+ (indexed to inflation)
estate_tax_exemption_history.source: IRS / Tax Foundation / Bipartisan Policy Center

wealth_tax_proposals.warren_ultra_millionaire_tax: 2% on net worth $50M-$1B; 3% on net worth above $1B
wealth_tax_proposals.wyden_billionaires_income_tax: Annual tax on unrealized capital gains for those with $1B+ net worth or $100M+ income for 3 consecutive years
wealth_tax_proposals.opposition: Deep-pocketed opposition from billionaires, financial industry lobbyists, and constitutional challenge arguments
wealth_tax_proposals.warren_quote: Billionaires and super millionaires talk a lot louder in Washington than everyone else. They make a lot more campaign contributions. They hire a lot more lobbyists.
wealth_tax_proposals.constitutional_questions: Opponents argue wealth taxes may violate the Constitution's 'direct tax' apportionment clause
wealth_tax_proposals.source: Penn Wharton Budget Model / Senate Finance Committee

tax_preparation_industry.overview: For over 20 years, Intuit (TurboTax) and H&R Block have spent tens of millions lobbying against free government-provided tax filing — a service available in most developed nations. Their success represents one of the clearest examples of industry lobbying preserving a market that could be eliminated by government action.

total_lobbying_against_free_filing.combined_since_2003: $93 million+ by tax prep companies since 2003 Free File Program launch
total_lobbying_against_free_filing.intuit_all_time: $43.3 million+ in total federal lobbying over two decades
total_lobbying_against_free_filing.hr_block_all_time: $42 million in total federal lobbying over two decades

recent_spending_surge.intuit_2023: Nearly $3.8 million — company record at the time

free_file_program_sabotage.launched: 2003 — public-private partnership between IRS and tax prep companies
free_file_program_sabotage.propublica_investigation: 20-year fight by Intuit to stop Americans from filing taxes for free (published 2019)
free_file_program_sabotage.tactics: Deliberately steering eligible users away from free filing options; deceptive advertising
free_file_program_sabotage.ftc_action: FTC found Intuit engaged in deceptive practices with 'free' TurboTax advertising

irs_direct_file.significance: First time taxpayers could file online directly with the IRS for free
irs_direct_file.industry_opposition: Well-funded lobbying campaign from tax prep industry + ideological resistance to expanding public services

irs_defunding.overview: Decades of budget cuts have gutted the IRS's ability to enforce tax law — particularly against wealthy individuals and corporations. The enforcement budget declined 23% (inflation-adjusted) from 2010, and audit rates for millionaires collapsed. Congress's $80 billion investment via the Inflation Reduction Act was systematically clawed back by Republicans.

budget_decline.annual_revenue_loss_from_cuts: ~$10 billion/year less collected from audits vs 2010 (inflation-adjusted)
budget_decline.staffing: Specialized agents leaving in droves, taking expertise in auditing complex corporate/billionaire returns

audit_rate_collapse.overall_rate_2019: 0.29% of individual returns audited (down from 0.89% in 2010)
audit_rate_collapse.millionaire_audit_decline: 80% less likely to be audited in 2018 vs 2011
audit_rate_collapse.over_5_million_income_decline: 66% decline in audit rate for returns TY2010-2019
audit_rate_collapse.disparity: EITC recipients (low-income) now about as likely to be audited as someone in the top 1%
audit_rate_collapse.reason: IRS shifted to 'cost-effective' correspondence audits on EITC recipients; complex corporate audits require specialized staff the IRS can no longer retain

ira_80_billion_funding.original_amount: ~$80 billion in mandatory IRS funding over 10 years (Inflation Reduction Act, August 2022)
ira_80_billion_funding.purpose: Modernize IRS, improve taxpayer services, crack down on wealthy tax evaders
ira_80_billion_funding.projected_revenue: $204 billion in additional revenue over 10 years (CBO estimate)

republican_clawback.fiscal_responsibility_act_2023: $1.4 billion rescinded immediately + agreement to claw back $20 billion
republican_clawback.house_republicans_initial_bill: First bill of 118th Congress was to rescind all $80 billion (H.R. 23, passed House January 2023)
republican_clawback.result: IRS enforcement boost that was supposed to last 10 years was effectively killed in under 3 years

who_benefits_from_defunding.primary_beneficiaries: Corporations and wealthy individuals who benefit most from reduced enforcement capacity
who_benefits_from_defunding.complexity_advantage: Complex tax schemes by billionaires and large corporations require specialized auditors the IRS can no longer employ

state_tax_competition.overview: State and local governments spend an estimated $45-90 billion annually on corporate tax incentives and economic development subsidies, often in a race-to-the-bottom competition for corporate relocations. The Amazon HQ2 bidding war epitomized this dynamic.

economic_development_subsidies.annual_total_estimate: $45-90 billion annually by state and local governments
economic_development_subsidies.good_jobs_first_estimate: Up to $90 billion/year given to corporations in economic development subsidies
economic_development_subsidies.megadeals: Subsidy packages worth $100 million+ increasingly common
economic_development_subsidies.effectiveness: Academic research suggests targeted incentives don't lead to broad-based economic growth

amazon_hq2_bidding_war.average_offer: $2.15 billion from cities, $6.75 billion from states (among 20 semifinalists) over 15 years
amazon_hq2_bidding_war.new_york_package: $1.525 billion in tax breaks + $325 million in cash grants + other incentives
amazon_hq2_bidding_war.virginia_package: Up to $750 million in taxpayer subsidies
amazon_hq2_bidding_war.new_york_cancellation: February 2019 — cancelled after strong grassroots opposition
amazon_hq2_bidding_war.amazon_lifetime_subsidies: $1 billion+ in state and local tax breaks over Amazon's lifetime (Good Jobs First)
amazon_hq2_bidding_war.source: Brookings / Good Jobs First / BuzzFeed News

salt_deduction_cap.tcja_cap: $10,000 cap on state and local tax deductions (2018-2025)
salt_deduction_cap.obbba_new_cap: $40,000 cap (phases down to $10,000 for incomes over $500,000)
salt_deduction_cap.who_benefits_from_higher_cap: Six-figure households in high-tax states (NY, CA, NJ, CT)
salt_deduction_cap.distributional_impact: Full repeal would cut taxes by $140,000+ for top 0.1%; 75% of benefit goes to those earning $430,000+
salt_deduction_cap.political_dynamics: SALT caucus — bipartisan coalition of NY, NJ, CA representatives; Rep. Mike Lawler (R-NY) key figure demanding SALT relief as condition for supporting tax legislation
salt_deduction_cap.source: Bipartisan Policy Center / Tax Policy Center / ITEP

academic_evidence.overview: Academic research consistently demonstrates that corporate lobbying on tax policy delivers extraordinary returns on investment, that the tax code functions as a shadow spending program larger than most federal agencies, and that political money systematically shapes tax policy in favor of wealthy interests.

university_of_kansas_study.finding: 22,000% return on lobbying investment for the 2004 repatriation tax holiday
university_of_kansas_study.detail: 93 firms spent $282.7 million lobbying and saved $62.5 billion collectively
university_of_kansas_study.implied_return: Every $1 spent on lobbying returned ~$220 in tax savings

sunlight_foundation_broader.finding: Companies that lobby more on taxes consistently pay lower effective tax rates
sunlight_foundation_broader.fixed_fortunes: Biggest corporate political interests spend billions on political influence, receive trillions in government benefits

tax_expenditures.total_2024: $1.9 trillion — more than spending on Social Security, Medicare, defense, or any other individual program
tax_expenditures.context: Tax expenditures function as a hidden spending program — reducing revenue rather than appropriating funds, making them less visible and less subject to annual review
tax_expenditures.source: Joint Committee on Taxation / Peter G. Peterson Foundation

corporate_tax_burden_trend.corporate_tax_share_of_gdp_decline: Corporate tax revenue has declined as a share of GDP for decades
corporate_tax_burden_trend.shift_to_individuals: Individual income taxes and payroll taxes now comprise a much larger share of federal revenue
corporate_tax_burden_trend.epi_finding: The TCJA overwhelmingly benefited the rich and corporations while overlooking working families

national_association_of_manufacturers.key_issues: Corporate rate reduction, R&D expensing, international tax reform

itep.name: Institute on Taxation and Economic Policy
itep.role: Primary researcher on corporate tax avoidance; publishes Fortune 500 tax analyses

americans_for_tax_fairness.role: Coalition of 420+ national and state organizations advocating for progressive tax reform

public_citizen.role: Published 'The Price of Zero' report linking zero-tax companies to political spending

the_fact_coalition.name: Financial Accountability and Corporate Transparency Coalition
the_fact_coalition.role: Advocates for corporate tax transparency and offshore haven reform

timeline.1995: Frank Luntz poll-tests 'death tax' rebranding; advises GOP never to say 'estate tax'
timeline.1996_2004: First wave of corporate inversions to Caribbean tax havens
timeline.2003: IRS Free File Program launched (public-private partnership with tax prep industry)
timeline.2004: Repatriation tax holiday — 93 firms spend $282.7M lobbying, save $62.5B (22,000% ROI)
timeline.2006: 18 wealthy families identified as funding coordinated estate tax repeal campaign
timeline.2012_2016: Second wave of corporate inversions — to Ireland and UK
timeline.2015: Medtronic-Covidien: $48B — largest completed corporate inversion in history
timeline.2016: Pfizer-Allergan: $160B inversion blocked by Obama Treasury rules; EU orders Apple to pay €13B in back taxes to Ireland
timeline.2017_q4: Lobbying filings on tax issues surge to 4,673 (from typical ~3,000); Business Roundtable spends $17.3M in Q4
timeline.2017_december: Tax Cuts and Jobs Act signed — corporate rate cut from 35% to 21%; $1.5T revenue cost over decade
timeline.2018: $806B in S&P 500 stock buybacks (55% jump); 91 Fortune 500 companies pay $0 in federal tax
timeline.2019: ProPublica publishes investigation of Intuit's 20-year fight against free tax filing
timeline.2020: 55 corporations pay $0 on $40B in combined profits; CARES Act adds new corporate tax breaks
timeline.2022_august: Inflation Reduction Act passes with $80B for IRS; Sinema demands removal of carried interest reform, killing $14B provision
timeline.2023_2025: Republicans claw back ~$41.8B (52%) of IRS funding through successive rescissions
timeline.2024_january: IRS Direct File launches in 13 states; OECD global minimum tax takes effect
timeline.2025_trump: Direct File program ended; U.S. withdraws from OECD global minimum tax framework
timeline.2025_july_4: One Big Beautiful Bill Act signed — makes TCJA individual provisions permanent, raises SALT cap to $40K, extends $4T in tax cuts
timeline.2026_january: OECD 'Side-by-Side' agreement effectively excludes U.S. multinationals from 15% global minimum tax

research_organizations: Institute on Taxation and Economic Policy (ITEP)
Center on Budget and Policy Priorities (CBPP)
